title: Narrow by-election win puts Qld Labor leader on notice
full_text:
Queensland Opposition Leader Steven Miles is on shaky ground after an 8 per cent primary swing against Labor in a state by-election on Saturday held within days of the federal budget.
Labor candidate Luke Richmond – a lawyer and Labor Party assistant state secretary – squeaked over the line with the help of Greens preferences in the working-class suburban Brisbane seat.
The Liberal National Party’s candidate, former Brisbane City councillor Fiona Hammond, secured 40.39 per cent of first-preference votes, compared with 30.69 per cent for Mr Richmond and nearly 15 per cent for the Greens’ Jess Lane, based on 78 per cent of votes counted by Sunday.
After preferences were distributed, the latest count showed 51.19 per cent of votes for Labor compared to 48.81 per cent for the LNP.
In a blow to Mr Miles’ hold on the state Labor leadership, the latest count showed an 8 per cent swing against Labor on first preferences, and 4 per cent on a two-party preferred count.
LNP Premier David Crisafulli said he was “humbled’’ by rising support for the LNP, even though his candidate had fallen “agonisingly short’’ of winning the safe Labor seat.
Deputy Premier Jarrod Beijie said voters had given Mr Miles an “absolute shellacking’’ and his leadership was in “absolute tatters’’.
Declaring victory, Mr Miles insisted the LNP had only gained ground because One Nation had failed to field a candidate. He said Stafford voters had also punished the LNP government for postponing an expansion of the local Prince Charles Hospital.
“Labor was never under any illusions that this would be a hard campaign, and the odds were stacked against us,’’ he said.
“This result is a direct result of David Crisafulli’s deal with One Nation for them not to run, and we will never know what the result would have been if they had run and not directed their supporters to vote for the LNP.
“Right-of-centre voters, after the deal with One Nation, weren’t left with many alternatives in a field of nine candidates, and so we have seen a splintering of the vote amongst other left-of-centre parties.’’
David Crisafulli has dismissed conflict of interest concerns over a year-long extramarital affair conducted by two of his now-ministers.
Mr Miles refused to answer repeated questions about the impact of the Albanese government’s federal budget on the by-election result, held just four days after federal Labor slashed spending on the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS), and clamped down on tax breaks for landlords.
“The Prime Minister, the Treasurer and the federal ministers are more than well-equipped to defend and explain their budget,’’ he said. “We are focused on state issues and, in particular, health care.
“(But) we know that at the end of the day, cost of living is hurting a lot of families.’’
Mr Miles said he retained the support of the Labor caucus and “our team is united’’. In an apparent show of factional unity, Labor’s deputy leader and Right faction leader, Cameron Dick, said that Mr Miles “is the best person to lead the team’’.
The most likely leadership challenger, opposition treasury spokesman Shannon Fentiman – a fellow member of he dominant Left faction – also pledged her support for Mr Miles to lead Labor to the 2028 state election.
“Steven is doing a fantastic job, we are all united behind him,’’ she said.
Ms Grace, who leads Labor’s Old Guard faction, also declared that “I support Steven’’.
Labor's new laws to limit who can access the NDIS scheme will mean more power to the Health Minister. The bill allows Mark Butler to put an instant stop to parts of the scheme if costs spiral. It will also require people to have exhausted all other treatment options before they are eligible to enter the scheme. On Thursday, the Senate halted the law after it was sent to an inquiry. The Greens claim it is “one of the most dangerous pieces of legislation”.
But Ms Grace, a former education minister, said Queensland Labor would keep a “close eye’’ on federal funding for disabled children, following the Albanese government’s cut to NDIS autism funding as well as disability loadings for schools.
“I don’t think any federal government is going to leave those children without the needs and supports that they require,’’ she said. “It may not come through the NDIS – my understanding is it comes through other channels.
“We’re looking forward to seeing how that transition happens, but we will be keeping a close eye on that to make sure that no child misses out, and I’m sure every federal member of parliament will be doing the same in their electorates.’’
Mr Miles, a former health minister, refused to say whether the Albanese government’s budget cut to subsidies for private health insurance for the over-65s would push more patients into the public hospital system.
“The argument that private health subsidies relieve that pressure is a contested space,’’ he said.
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
Document AUSTOL0020260517em5h000xd
summary: Queensland Labor leader Steven Miles faces pressure after a narrow by-election win, with an 8% swing against Labor. The result raises questions about his leadership and the impact of federal budget cuts on state issues.
author: SARAH ELKS and NATASHA BITA
heading: General Insurance
key_points: Labor candidate Luke Richmond won the by-election with 51.19% after preferences.
LNP candidate Fiona Hammond received 40.39% of first-preference votes.
An 8% swing against Labor raises concerns for Miles' leadership.
Deputy Premier Jarrod Beijie criticized Miles' leadership as being in 'absolute tatters'.
Miles claims the LNP's gains were due to One Nation not running a candidate.
ticker_line: Labor leader Steven Miles under pressure after by-election
